summary:
Antoine Lavoisier and the Chemical Revolution (c. 1770–1794 AD) transformed chemistry from an unsystematic collection of observations and alchemical traditions into a rigorous experimental science based on precise measurement, conservation of mass, and a new theoretical framework — the 'Chemical Revolution' that is sometimes compared to the Copernican Revolution in astronomy for its comprehensive replacement of the existing explanatory system. Lavoisier, a French nobleman and tax farmer who dedicated his substantial private wealth to laboratory equipment of unprecedented precision, demonstrated through carefully quantified experiments that combustion involved the combination of substances with oxygen (a gas he named, from the Greek for 'acid-former') rather than the release of the fictional 'phlogiston' substance that had explained fire for a century.

Lavoisier's conservation of mass principle — that matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions, only rearranged (demonstrated by careful weighing of reactants and products) — provided chemistry with its foundational quantitative law. His Traité Élémentaire de Chimie (Elementary Treatise on Chemistry, 1789) was the first modern chemistry textbook: it defined the chemical element as the simplest substance that could not be broken down further by chemical means, listed 33 elements (some incorrectly, including 'caloric' for heat), and introduced a systematic chemical nomenclature that replaced the confusing alchemical names (vitriol, lunar caustic, etc.) with rational names based on composition (sulfuric acid, silver nitrate) — most of which remain in use. Lavoisier was guillotined during the [[event:107|Reign of Terror]] in 1794 (reportedly because of his role as a tax farmer, though his scientific work drew accusations of hoarding gunpowder); the mathematician Lagrange remarked: 'It took them only a moment to cut off that head, and a hundred years may not produce another like it.'
